title: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases

items.subtopic: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases | CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)
items.title: Recognizing Patterns of Muscle Weakness in Myopathies
items.content: Understanding the pattern of muscle weakness is crucial for narrowing differential diagnoses in myopathies. Most myopathies present with proximal, symmetric limb weakness while sparing facial muscles, known as limb-girdle weakness. Distinct patterns like facial and scapular winging suggest facioscapulohumeral dystrophy, whereas distal limb weakness with hand grip myotonia is indicative of myotonic dystrophy type 1.
keyPoints: Most myopathies present with proximal, symmetric limb weakness with preserved reflexes and sensation.
Facial muscles are typically spared in most myopathies (limb-girdle weakness).
Facioscapulohumeral dystrophy is characterized by facial weakness and scapular winging.
Myotonic dystrophy type 1 presents with facial and distal limb weakness along with hand grip myotonia.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases | CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.subtopic: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases | CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)
items.title: Differentiating Pathologic Fatigability from Asthenia
items.content: Pathologic fatigability, seen in neuromuscular disorders affecting energy production or transmission, differs from asthenia. It is characterized by an inability to maintain muscle force and is accompanied by abnormal clinical or laboratory findings. In contrast, asthenia involves general tiredness without specific neuromuscular abnormalities.
keyPoints: Pathologic fatigability occurs in disorders of neuromuscular transmission and energy production defects (glycolysis, lipid metabolism, mitochondrial).
Asthenia is characterized by avoidance of physical activities, daytime sleepiness, frequent naps, and difficulty concentrating.
Fatigue without supportive clinical or laboratory findings rarely indicates a primary muscle disease.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases | CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.subtopic: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases | CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)
items.title: Identifying Functional Impairments in Myopathies
items.content: Functional assessments, such as observing the Gower sign or gait abnormalities, can reveal specific patterns of muscle weakness. The Gower sign indicates proximal muscle weakness, while a waddling gait suggests weak hip muscles. Hyperlordotic posture and genu recurvatum are indicative of trunk/hip and quadriceps muscle weaknesses, respectively.
keyPoints: The Gower sign is useful for identifying proximal muscle weakness.
A hyperlordotic posture may indicate combined trunk and hip weakness.
Waddling gait results from weak hip muscles preventing hip drop or dip.
Genu recurvatum (back-kneeing) suggests quadriceps muscle weakness.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases | CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.subtopic: CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2) | Eye Muscle Weakness (Ptosis/Ophthalmoparesis)
items.title: Understanding Ptosis with and without Ophthalmoparesis
items.content: Eye muscle weakness can manifest as ptosis alone or in combination with ophthalmoparesis. The underlying causes differ based on the presence of ophthalmoparesis, guiding diagnostic considerations.
keyPoints: Ptosis without ophthalmoparesis is associated with myotonic dystrophy, congenital myopathies, and neuromuscular junction disorders.
Ptosis with ophthalmoparesis can indicate conditions such as oculopharyngeal dystrophy, mitochondrial myopathy, hIBM type 3, and neuromuscular junction disorders.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2) | Eye Muscle Weakness (Ptosis/Ophthalmoparesis) | Episodic Weakness or Myoglobinuria

items.subtopic: CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2) | Eye Muscle Weakness (Ptosis/Ophthalmoparesis) | Episodic Weakness or Myoglobinuria
items.title: Identifying Causes of Exercise-Related and Unrelated Episodic Weakness/Myoglobinuria
items.content: Episodic weakness or myoglobinuria can be exercise-related, not related to exercise, or delayed/unrelated. Recognizing these patterns aids in differential diagnosis.
keyPoints: Exercise-related episodic weakness/myoglobinuria is linked to glycogenoses (e.g., McArdle disease), lipid disorders (e.g., CPT2 deficiency), and mitochondrial myopathies.
Not exercise-related causes include RYR1 mutations leading to malignant hyperthermia, episodic rhabdomyolysis/myoglobinuria, and atypical periodic paralysis.

Delayed or unrelated episodes can be due to periodic paralysis types (e.g., hereditary hyper- or hypokalemic), drugs/toxins like statins, prolonged eccentric exercise, inflammatory causes, and NMJ disorders.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2) | Eye Muscle Weakness (Ptosis/Ophthalmoparesis) | Episodic Weakness or Myoglobinuria

items.subtopic: Muscle Stiffness/Decreased Ability to Relax
items.title: Myotonia: Mechanisms and Clinical Features
items.content: Myotonia is characterized by prolonged muscle contraction followed by slow relaxation, often triggered by voluntary or mechanical stimulation. It's seen in conditions like myotonic dystrophy types 1 and 2, myotonia congenita (chloride channel disorder), and sodium channelopathies such as hyperkalemic periodic paralysis and paramyotonia congenita.
keyPoints: Myotonia follows muscle activation and causes difficulty releasing objects after a firm grasp.
DM1 typically involves distal weakness with myotonia, while DM2 affects proximal muscles more.
Paramyotonia congenita (PC) is unique as repetitive activity worsens myotonia.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Muscle Stiffness/Decreased Ability to Relax

items.subtopic: Muscle Stiffness/Decreased Ability to Relax
items.title: Stiff-Person Syndrome: Diagnosis and Pathophysiology
items.content: Stiff-person syndrome involves involuntary muscle contractions, primarily in axial and proximal lower extremity muscles, leading to a stiff gait. It is associated with serum antibodies against glutamic acid decarboxylase found in about two-thirds of cases.
keyPoints: Characterized by spontaneous motor neuron discharges causing stiffness and spasms.
Gait becomes stiff and labored; hyperlordosis may be present.
Serum antibodies against glutamic acid decarboxylase are present in ~2/3 of cases.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Muscle Stiffness/Decreased Ability to Relax

items.subtopic: Muscle Stiffness/Decreased Ability to Relax
items.title: Acquired Neuromyotonia (Isaacs' Syndrome): Clinical Presentation and Pathophysiology
items.content: Acquired neuromyotonia is marked by hyperexcitability of peripheral nerves, leading to continuous muscle fiber activity, fasciculations, myokymia, and impaired relaxation. It's mediated by antibodies targeting voltage-gated potassium channels.
keyPoints: Involves continuous muscle fiber activity with widespread fasciculations and myokymia.
Muscle stiffness is prominent in the legs, with increased sweating of extremities.
Antibodies against voltage-gated potassium channels mediate peripheral nerve hyperexcitability.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Muscle Stiffness/Decreased Ability to Relax

items.subtopic: LABORATORY EVALUATION (part 1)
items.title: CK Levels and Muscle Damage
items.content: CK is the most sensitive marker for muscle damage, with the MM isoenzyme predominating in skeletal muscle. Elevated CK can occur without pathology due to genetic factors or physical exertion.
keyPoints: CK is the most sensitive measure of muscle damage.
MM isoenzyme predominates in skeletal muscle; CK-MB indicates cardiac muscle involvement.
Elevated serum CK may be seen in normal individuals after strenuous activity, trauma, prolonged muscle cramp, or generalized seizure.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · LABORATORY EVALUATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY EVALUATION (part 1)
items.title: Electrodiagnostic Studies for Myopathy
items.content: EMG and nerve conduction studies help differentiate myopathies from other neuromuscular disorders. Specific EMG findings can indicate active necrosis or membrane instability in inflammatory myopathies.
keyPoints: Routine NCS are typically normal in myopathies; reduced amplitudes may be seen in atrophied muscles.
Needle EMG shows irritability with active necrosis or muscle membrane instability (inflammatory myopathies, dystrophies).
Short-duration, small-amplitude, polyphasic MUAPs are characteristic of myopathies.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · LABORATORY EVALUATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY EVALUATION (part 1)
items.title: Role of Imaging in Myopathy Diagnosis
items.content: MRI and ultrasound can assess muscle involvement patterns, aiding diagnosis when clinical examination or EMG are inconclusive. They help guide biopsy decisions.
keyPoints: MRI is sensitive for early detection of muscle involvement, e.g., vastus lateralis/medialis in inclusion body myositis.
Imaging guides biopsy site selection, especially in non-typical muscles with EMG abnormalities.
Ultrasound is more accessible and cost-effective compared to MRI.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · LABORATORY EVALUATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY EVALUATION (part 2) | HEREDITARY MYOPATHIES
items.title: Interpreting the Forearm Exercise Test for Glycolytic Defects
items.content: The forearm exercise test is used to identify defects in glycolysis by measuring lactic acid and ammonia levels post-exercise. A significant rise in these metabolites indicates normal muscle metabolism, whereas an absence or insufficient increase may suggest a glycolytic defect.
keyPoints: A three- to fourfold rise of lactic acid is typical after exercise.
Ammonia should also rise with exercise as a control measure.
In myophosphorylase deficiency and other glycolytic defects, the expected lactic acid rise may be absent or below normal.
Lack of effort will result in no increase in either lactic acid or ammonia levels.
Selective failure to increase ammonia could indicate myoadenylate deaminase deficiency.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · LABORATORY EVALUATION (part 2) | HEREDITARY MYOPATHIES

items.subtopic: LABORATORY EVALUATION (part 2) | HEREDITARY MYOPATHIES
items.title: Role of Muscle Biopsy in Hereditary Myopathies
items.content: Muscle biopsy remains a valuable diagnostic tool for hereditary myopathies, especially when genetic testing is nondiagnostic. It helps establish specific diagnoses by examining clinically affected but not severely compromised muscles.
keyPoints: Muscle biopsy is less frequently used now due to advances in genetic testing.
It is useful when genetic tests do not provide a diagnosis.
Biopsy should target muscles that are clinically involved but not too severely impaired (e.g., grade 4 out of 5 strength).
Can establish specific diagnoses for many disorders.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · LABORATORY EVALUATION (part 2) | HEREDITARY MYOPATHIES

items.subtopic: DUCHENNE AND BECKER MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY (DMD AND BMD)
items.title: Incidence and Onset of DMD vs. BMD
items.content: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD) is more common than Becker Muscular Dystrophy (BMD), with an incidence of 1 in 3000 male births for DMD compared to ~5 per 100,000 for BMD. DMD typically presents early in childhood with proximal muscle weakness and difficulty running, while BMD onset can range from ages 5-15 or even later.
keyPoints: DMD incidence: 1 in 3000 male births
BMD incidence: ~5 per 100,000
Early symptoms of DMD include difficulty climbing stairs and never running well
Onset for BMD typically between ages 5-15 but can occur later
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · DUCHENNE AND BECKER MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY (DMD AND BMD)

items.subtopic: DUCHENNE AND BECKER MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY (DMD AND BMD)
items.title: Diagnostic Approach and Genetic Testing in DMD and BMD
items.content: Genetic testing has largely replaced muscle biopsies for diagnosing DMD and BMD due to its less invasive nature, lower cost, and routine availability. Most cases involve mutations that do not alter the translational reading frame of mRNA, explaining the presence of altered dystrophin in BMD.
keyPoints: Genetic testing is preferred over muscle biopsies for diagnosis
~95% of mutations are 'in-frame', allowing some dystrophin production
Western blot analysis shows absent dystrophin in DMD and reduced levels or size in BMD
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · DUCHENNE AND BECKER MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY (DMD AND BMD)

items.subtopic: Duchenne and Becker Muscular Dystrophy | LIMB-GIRDLE MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY
items.title: Management of Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD)
items.content: Glucocorticoids are used to slow the progression of DMD, though their efficacy in BMD is not well studied. Cardiomyopathy management is crucial due to its role in mortality. Emerging therapies include short oligonucleotides for exon skipping and small molecules for read-through mutations.
keyPoints: Glucocorticoids slow progression in DMD.
Cardiomyopathy often causes death; requires cardiologist follow-up.
Short oligonucleotides may benefit selected DMD cases by allowing dystrophin expression.
Small molecules might enable read-through of protein-truncating mutations in some DMD cases.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Duchenne and Becker Muscular Dystrophy | LIMB-GIRDLE MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY

items.subtopic: Duchenne and Becker Muscular Dystrophy | LIMB-GIRDLE MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY
items.title: Diagnosis and Classification of Limb-Girdle Muscular Dystrophies (LGMDs)
items.content: LGMDs are genetically heterogeneous, affecting males and females equally with onset from the first to fourth decade. Diagnosis involves elevated CK levels, myopathic EMG, muscle biopsy, and genetic testing. The ENMC classification system differentiates LGMD based on inheritance patterns and specific criteria.
keyPoints: LGMDs affect both genders equally; onset ranges from 1st to 4th decade.
Diagnosis requires elevated CK, myopathic EMG, muscle biopsy, and genetic testing.
ENMC classification: autosomal dominant as LGMD 'D', recessive as LGMD 'R'.
Criteria for classification include two unrelated families with similar phenotype.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Duchenne and Becker Muscular Dystrophy | LIMB-GIRDLE MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY

items.subtopic: Duchenne and Becker Muscular Dystrophy | LIMB-GIRDLE MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY
items.title: Prevalence and Subtypes of Limb-Girdle Muscular Dystrophies (LGMDs)
items.content: The prevalence of LGMD is 1.63 per 100,000. Common adult-onset subtypes include calpainopathy, FKRP deficiency, and anoctaminopathy. Calpainopathy is prevalent in individuals from Spain, France, Italy, and Great Britain, while anoctaminopathy shows early calf atrophy.
keyPoints: Prevalence of LGMD: 1.63 per 100,000 (range 0.56–5.75).
Common adult-onset subtypes: calpainopathy, FKRP deficiency, anoctaminopathy.
Calpainopathy prevalent in Spain, France, Italy, Great Britain; lacks cardiac/lung involvement.
Anoctaminopathy shows early calf atrophy and weakness.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Duchenne and Becker Muscular Dystrophy | LIMB-GIRDLE MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY

items.subtopic: EMERY-DREIFUSS MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY
items.title: Understanding the Genetic Basis of EMERYS-DREIFUSS MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY (EDMD)
items.content: EDMD is a heterogeneous group of disorders with at least seven subtypes linked to mutations in different genes. These include EMD, LMNA, SYNE1, SYNE2, FHL1, and TMEM43, affecting proteins like emerin, lamin A/C, nesprins, FHL1, and LUMA. The genetic inheritance patterns vary: X-linked for EMD and FHL1 mutations, autosomal dominant for LMNA, SYNE1, SYNE2, and TMEM43, and autosomal recessive for some LMNA mutations.
keyPoints: EDMD has at least seven subtypes linked to specific gene mutations.
X-linked inheritance is seen with EMD (EDMD1) and FHL1 (EDMD6).
Autosomal dominant forms include LMNA (EDMD2, EDMD3), SYNE1 (EDMD4), SYNE2 (EDMD5), and TMEM43 (EDMD7).
Autosomal recessive form involves some LMNA mutations.
Proteins affected are emerin, lamin A/C, nesprin-1, nesprin-2, FHL1, and LUMA.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · EMERY-DREIFUSS MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY

items.subtopic: EMERY-DREIFUSS MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY
items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Diagnostic Features of EMERYS-DREIFUSS MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY (EDMD)
items.content: EDMD is characterized by early childhood contractures at the elbows, ankles, and neck, followed by muscle weakness starting in humeral and peroneal muscles. Cardiomyopathy, including atrial fibrillation, standstill, and heart block, poses a life-threatening risk with potential for sudden death. Female carriers of X-linked EDMD may also develop cardiomyopathy. Diagnostic features include slightly elevated serum CK levels, myopathic EMG findings, nonspecific dystrophic muscle biopsy results, absent emerin staining in X-linked cases, and ECG abnormalities.
keyPoints: Contractures appear early at elbows, ankles, neck, followed by humeral and peroneal muscle weakness.
Cardiomyopathy is a major risk with potential for sudden death.
Female carriers of X-linked EDMD may develop cardiomyopathy.
Serum CK levels are usually slightly elevated; EMG shows myopathic changes.
Muscle biopsy reveals nonspecific dystrophic features, absent emerin staining in X-linked cases.
ECGs show atrial and atrioventricular rhythm disturbances.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · EMERY-DREIFUSS MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY

items.subtopic: Emery-Dreifuss Muscular Dystrophy | MYOTONIC DYSTROPHY
items.title: Management of Cardiomyopathy in Emery-Dreifuss Muscular Dystrophy
items.content: Cardiomyopathy and arrhythmias are critical complications in Emery-Dreifuss muscular dystrophy that require early intervention to prevent life-threatening events.
keyPoints: Supportive care for neuromuscular disability includes ambulatory aids if necessary.
Management of cardiomyopathy and arrhythmias may be lifesaving, with interventions like defibrillators or pacemakers.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Emery-Dreifuss Muscular Dystrophy | MYOTONIC DYSTROPHY

items.subtopic: Emery-Dreifuss Muscular Dystrophy | MYOTONIC DYSTROPHY
items.title: Clinical Features of Myotonic Dystrophy Type 1 (DM1)
items.content: DM1 presents with a wide range of systemic features, including distinct facial appearance and myotonia, which are important for diagnosis.
keyPoints: Patients may have a 'hatchet-faced' appearance due to muscle atrophy.
Myotonia is usually apparent by age 5, demonstrated by percussion of the thenar eminence or finger relaxation test.
ECG abnormalities include first-degree heart block and potential for complete heart block and sudden death.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Emery-Dreifuss Muscular Dystrophy | MYOTONIC DYSTROPHY

items.subtopic: Emery-Dreifuss Muscular Dystrophy | MYOTONIC DYSTROPHY
items.title: Genetic Basis and Diagnostic Features of Myotonic Dystrophies
items.content: DM1 and DM2 are autosomal dominant disorders with distinct genetic mutations that lead to systemic manifestations through toxic gain-of-function mechanisms.
keyPoints: DM1 involves a CTG trinucleotide repeat expansion in the DMPK gene, leading to genetic anticipation.
Congenital myotonic dystrophy occurs in ~25% of infants born to affected mothers.
DM2 is caused by a CCTG repeat expansion in intron 1 of the CNBP gene.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Emery-Dreifuss Muscular Dystrophy | MYOTONIC DYSTROPHY

items.subtopic: Myotonic Dystrophy | FACIOSCAPULOHUMERAL (FSHD) MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY
items.title: Management of Myotonia in DM1 and DM2
items.content: In myotonic dystrophies, particularly DM1 and DM2, myotonia can be managed with mexiletine when symptomatic. For significant arrhythmias, a cardiac pacemaker or implantable cardioverter defibrillator is recommended.
keyPoints: Mexiletine may help manage bothersome myotonia in DM1 and DM2.
Consider cardiac pacemakers or implantable cardioverter defibrillators for patients with significant arrhythmias.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Myotonic Dystrophy | FACIOSCAPULOHUMERAL (FSHD) MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY

items.subtopic: Myotonic Dystrophy | FACIOSCAPULOHUMERAL (FSHD) MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY
items.title: Clinical Features and Diagnosis of FSHD
items.content: FSHD typically presents in childhood or young adulthood with facial weakness, scapular winging, and muscle weakness. The prevalence is about 5 per 100,000 individuals. Serum CK may be normal or mildly elevated; EMG and biopsy show nonspecific changes.
keyPoints: FSHD has a prevalence of ~5 per 100,000 individuals.
Initial manifestations often include facial weakness and scapular winging.
Serum CK levels can be normal or mildly elevated in FSHD.
EMG and muscle biopsy show nonspecific abnormalities.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Myotonic Dystrophy | FACIOSCAPULOHUMERAL (FSHD) MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY

items.subtopic: Facioscapulohumeral Muscular Dystrophy | OCULOPHARYNGEAL DYSTROPHY (OPMD)
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of OPMD
items.content: Oculopharyngeal Muscular Dystrophy (OPMD) is characterized by progressive external ophthalmoplegia, presenting with ptosis and dysphagia typically in the fourth to sixth decade. Diagnosis is supported by elevated serum CK levels and EMG findings. Muscle biopsies show rimmed vacuoles and 8.5-nm tubular filaments on electron microscopy.
keyPoints: OPMD presents with ptosis, dysphagia, and progressive external ophthalmoplegia in the fourth to sixth decade.
Serum CK level may be two to three times normal.
EMG shows myopathic changes; muscle biopsies reveal rimmed vacuoles and 8.5-nm tubular filaments.
OPMD is autosomal dominant with high incidence in certain populations (e.g., French-Canadians, individuals of Spanish ancestry, Ashkenazi Jews).
Molecular defect involves expansion of a polyalanine repeat tract in the PABP2 gene.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Facioscapulohumeral Muscular Dystrophy | OCULOPHARYNGEAL DYSTROPHY (OPMD)

items.subtopic: Facioscapulohumeral Muscular Dystrophy | OCULOPHARYNGEAL DYSTROPHY (OPMD)
items.title: Clinical Features and Pathophysiology of OPMD
items.content: OPMD is marked by progressive external ophthalmoplegia, ptosis, dysphagia, and mild proximal muscle weakness. The disease involves abnormal folding of PABP2 due to GCG repeat expansion, leading to intranuclear aggregates that cause direct toxicity or sequestration of essential proteins.
keyPoints: OPMD presents with progressive external ophthalmoplegia, ptosis, dysphagia, and mild proximal muscle weakness.
Extraocular muscle impairment is less prominent initially but may worsen over time.
Pathophysiology involves GCG repeat expansion in PABP2 causing abnormal protein folding and intranuclear aggregates.
Potential mechanisms include direct toxicity of aggregates or sequestration of essential proteins.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Facioscapulohumeral Muscular Dystrophy | OCULOPHARYNGEAL DYSTROPHY (OPMD)

items.subtopic: Oculopharyngeal Dystrophy | OCULOPHARYNGEAL DISTAL MYOPATHY (OPDM) | Oculopharyngeal Distal Myopathy | DISTAL MYOPATHIES/DYSTROPHIES
items.title: Clinical Features and Genetic Basis of OPDM
items.content: OPDM presents with adult-onset ptosis, external ophthalmoplegia, facial muscle weakness, distal limb muscle weakness and atrophy, and pharyngeal involvement leading to dysphagia and dysarthria. It is genetically heterogeneous due to trinucleotide repeat expansions in specific genes.
keyPoints: OPDM characterized by adult-onset ptosis, external ophthalmoplegia, facial muscle weakness, distal limb muscle weakness and atrophy, pharyngeal involvement (dysphagia, dysarthria).
Genetically heterogeneous autosomal disorder caused by trinucleotide repeat expansions in LRP12 (OPDM1), GIPC1 (OPDM2), NOTCH2NLC (OPDM3), RILPL1 (OPMD4).
CGG repeat expansion in NOTCH2NLC also causes neuronal intranuclear hyaline inclusion disease and other neurodegenerative diseases.
RNA-mediated sequestration of RNA-binding proteins and altered translation of proteins are implicated.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Oculopharyngeal Dystrophy | OCULOPHARYNGEAL DISTAL MYOPATHY (OPDM) | Oculopharyngeal Distal Myopathy | DISTAL MYOPATHIES/DYSTROPHIES

items.subtopic: Oculopharyngeal Dystrophy | OCULOPHARYNGEAL DISTAL MYOPATHY (OPDM) | Oculopharyngeal Distal Myopathy | DISTAL MYOPATHIES/DYSTROPHIES
items.title: Laboratory Features and Differential Diagnosis of OPDM
items.content: Serum CK levels in OPDM are normal or mildly elevated. EMG shows myopathic changes, and muscle biopsies reveal dystrophic features with rimmed vacuoles and intranuclear inclusions immunostained by anti-phospho-p62/SQSTM1 antibodies.
keyPoints: Serum CK levels in OPDM are normal or only mildly elevated.
EMG is myopathic.
Muscle biopsies reveal dystrophic features including muscle fibers with rimmed vacuoles and intranuclear inclusions immunostained by anti-phospho-p62/SQSTM1 antibodies.
Similar intranuclear inclusions found on skin biopsies.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Oculopharyngeal Dystrophy | OCULOPHARYNGEAL DISTAL MYOPATHY (OPDM) | Oculopharyngeal Distal Myopathy | DISTAL MYOPATHIES/DYSTROPHIES

items.subtopic: Distal Myopathies | MULTISYSTEM PROTEINOPATHIES (MSP) | SPORADIC LATE-ONSET NEMALINE MYOPATHY
items.title: Management and Complications of Distal Myopathies
items.content: Occupational therapy is recommended for managing loss of hand function in distal myopathies, while ankle-foot orthoses can support lower limb muscles. Multisystem proteinopathies (MSPs) may present with cardiomyopathy, respiratory failure, and other systemic features such as amyotrophic lateral sclerosis or frontotemporal dementia.
keyPoints: Occupational therapy for hand function loss
Ankle-foot orthoses support lower limb muscles
Cardiomyopathy (congestive heart failure or arrhythmias) may require medical management in MFMs
Laing-type distal myopathy can also be associated with cardiomyopathy
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Distal Myopathies | MULTISYSTEM PROTEINOPATHIES (MSP) | SPORADIC LATE-ONSET NEMALINE MYOPATHY

items.subtopic: Distal Myopathies | MULTISYSTEM PROTEINOPATHIES (MSP) | SPORADIC LATE-ONSET NEMALINE MYOPATHY
items.title: Characteristics and Diagnostic Approach for Sporadic Late-Onset Nemaline Myopathy (SLONM)
items.content: SLONM, a non-genetic disorder presenting after age 40 with proximal extremity weakness, can involve axial myopathy or cardiomyopathy. Diagnosis is supported by normal to mildly elevated serum CK levels and EMG findings of irritable myopathy. Muscle biopsy reveals nemaline rods visible on electron microscopy.
keyPoints: SLONM presents after age 40 with proximal extremity weakness
May involve axial myopathy, isolated head drop, or bent spine syndrome
Serum CK is usually normal or mildly elevated
EMG shows signs of an irritable myopathy
About 50% associated with monoclonal gammopathy (IgG or IgA)
Muscle biopsy reveals nemaline rods visible on electron microscopy
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Distal Myopathies | MULTISYSTEM PROTEINOPATHIES (MSP) | SPORADIC LATE-ONSET NEMALINE MYOPATHY

items.subtopic: SLONM | DISORDERS OF MUSCLE ENERGY METABOLISM | GLYCOGEN STORAGE AND GLYCOLYTIC DEFECTS
items.title: Management of SLONM with Immunosuppressive Therapies and Stem Cell Transplantation
items.content: Some patients with Sporadic Late-Onset Nemaline Myopathy (SLONM) respond to intravenous immunoglobulin or other immunosuppressive therapies. Additionally, autologous stem cell transplantation has been beneficial in some SLONM cases, particularly those associated with monoclonal gammopathy.
keyPoints: Intravenous immunoglobulin and immunosuppressive therapies can be effective for some SLONM patients.
Autologous stem cell transplantation may benefit SLONM patients with a monoclonal gammopathy.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · SLONM | DISORDERS OF MUSCLE ENERGY METABOLISM | GLYCOGEN STORAGE AND GLYCOLYTIC DEFECTS

items.subtopic: GLYCOGEN STORAGE AND GLYCOLYTIC DEFECTS
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of McArdle Disease
items.content: McArdle disease, caused by mutations in the PYGM gene leading to myophosphorylase deficiency, presents with muscle pain and stiffness typically beginning in adolescence. Myoglobinuria can occur during severe episodes. Exercise tolerance may improve with a slow warm-up or brief rest periods, allowing for 'second-wind' phenomenon. Serum CK levels >100 times normal are expected with myoglobinuria. Diagnosis is confirmed by genetic testing.
keyPoints: McArdle disease results from PYGM gene mutations causing myophosphorylase deficiency.
Symptoms include muscle pain and stiffness, often starting in adolescence.
Exercise tolerance can improve with a slow warm-up or brief rest ('second-wind' phenomenon).
Serum CK levels may exceed 100 times normal during myoglobinuria episodes.
Definitive diagnosis is made via genetic testing.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · SLONM | DISORDERS OF MUSCLE ENERGY METABOLISM | GLYCOGEN STORAGE AND GLYCOLYTIC DEFECTS

items.subtopic: GLYCOGEN STORAGE AND GLYCOLYTIC DEFECTS
items.title: Clinical Forms and Diagnosis of Pompe Disease
items.content: Pompe disease, caused by $\alpha$-glucosidase deficiency, has three clinical forms: infantile, childhood, and adult. The infantile form presents with severe muscle weakness, cardiomegaly, hepatomegaly, and respiratory insufficiency, often leading to death by 1.5 years. Childhood-onset resembles DMD with delayed motor milestones. Adult-onset may present as late as the seventh decade, sometimes only with ventilatory weakness. Serum CK levels are 2–10 times normal in infantile or childhood onset but can be normal in adult cases. Diagnosis is confirmed by genetic testing.
keyPoints: Pompe disease results from $\alpha$-glucosidase deficiency and has three clinical forms: infantile, childhood, and adult.
Infantile form presents with severe muscle weakness, cardiomegaly, hepatomegaly, respiratory insufficiency; death usually by 1.5 years.
Childhood-onset resembles DMD with delayed motor milestones.
Adult-onset may present late with ventilatory weakness as the initial symptom in some cases.
Serum CK levels are elevated (2–10 times normal) in infantile or childhood onset but can be normal in adult cases.
Diagnosis is confirmed by genetic testing.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · SLONM | DISORDERS OF MUSCLE ENERGY METABOLISM | GLYCOGEN STORAGE AND GLYCOLYTIC DEFECTS

items.subtopic: LIPID AS AN ENERGY SOURCE AND ASSOCIATED DEFECTS
items.title: CPT2 Deficiency: Recognizing Recurrent Myoglobinuria and Exercise-Induced Muscle Pain
items.content: Carnitine Palmitoyltransferase 2 (CPT2) deficiency is the most common recognizable cause of recurrent myoglobinuria, typically presenting in teenage years or early twenties. It manifests with muscle pain and myoglobinuria after prolonged exercise, fasting, or infections. Unlike glycolytic defects, muscle cramps occur only when utilization limits are exceeded.
keyPoints: Most common recognizable cause of recurrent myoglobinuria
Onset usually in teenage years or early twenties
Muscle pain and myoglobinuria after prolonged exercise, fasting, or infections
Up to 20% do not exhibit myoglobinuria
Normal strength between attacks
Distinguishing feature: normal rise of venous lactate during forearm exercise (differentiates from glycolytic defects)
Serum CK levels and EMG findings usually normal between episodes
Muscle biopsy typically normal between attacks, no lipid accumulation
Diagnosis requires direct measurement of muscle CPT or genetic testing
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · LIPID AS AN ENERGY SOURCE AND ASSOCIATED DEFECTS

items.subtopic: LIPID AS AN ENERGY SOURCE AND ASSOCIATED DEFECTS
items.title: Management Challenges in CPT2 Deficiency: Dietary Interventions and Exercise Tolerance
items.content: In managing CPT2 deficiency, dietary interventions such as frequent meals with a low-fat, high-carbohydrate diet or substituting medium-chain triglycerides have not proven beneficial. This highlights the challenge in improving exercise tolerance through dietary modifications alone.
keyPoints: Frequent meals and low-fat, high-carbohydrate diet do not improve exercise tolerance
Substituting medium-chain triglycerides is also ineffective
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · LIPID AS AN ENERGY SOURCE AND ASSOCIATED DEFECTS

items.subtopic: MITOCHONDRIAL MYOPATHIES (part 1)
items.title: Understanding the Genetic Basis of Mitochondrial Myopathies
items.content: Mitochondrial myopathies are characterized by mutations in mitochondrial DNA, which is maternally inherited. This distinct genetic composition results in unique clinical manifestations and inheritance patterns.
keyPoints: Human mtDNA consists of 16,569 base pairs coding for essential respiratory chain enzymes.
Mitochondrial genes are almost exclusively derived from the mother due to maternal inheritance.
KSS is caused by single mtDNA deletions, with a common deletion removing 4977 bp.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · MITOCHONDRIAL MYOPATHIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MITOCHONDRIAL MYOPATHIES (part 1)
items.title: Clinical Features and Diagnosis of Kearns-Sayre Syndrome
items.content: Kearns-Sayre Syndrome presents with a triad of onset before age 20, CPEO, and pigmentary retinopathy. Additional features include cardiac conduction defects and endocrine abnormalities.
keyPoints: Cardiac disease in KSS includes syncopal attacks and complete heart block, affecting ~20% of patients.
Serum CK and lactate levels are normal or slightly elevated; FGF-21 and GDF-15 may be elevated.
Prophylactic pacemaker implantation is indicated for ECGs showing a bifascicular block.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · MITOCHONDRIAL MYOPATHIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MITOCHONDRIAL MYOPATHIES (part 1)
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Progressive External Ophthalmoplegia
items.content: Progressive External Ophthalmoplegia can result from mutations in nuclear or mitochondrial DNA, presenting with ptosis, ophthalmoplegia, and muscle weakness.
keyPoints: PEO onset is usually after puberty; symptoms include fatigue, exercise intolerance, and dysphagia.
Serum CK and lactate levels are normal or mildly elevated; EMG shows myopathic changes.
Common genetic causes include mutations in ANT1, twinkle gene (C10orf2), and POLG1.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · MITOCHONDRIAL MYOPATHIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MITOCHONDRIAL MYOPATHIES (part 2)
items.title: Recognizing MERRF: Key Genetic Mutation and Clinical Features
items.content: MERRF, or Myoclonic Epilepsy with Ragged Red Fibers, is primarily caused by a maternally inherited point mutation in mitochondrial tRNA genes. The most common mutation involves an A to G substitution at nucleotide 8344 of tRNA lysine (A8344G tRNA $^{lys}$), found in 80% of patients. Clinical features include epilepsy and muscle biopsy findings of ragged red fibers.
keyPoints: MERRF is caused by maternally inherited point mutations of mitochondrial tRNA genes.
The most common mutation is an A to G substitution at nucleotide 8344 of tRNA lysine (A8344G tRNA $^{lys}$), found in 80% of patients.
Clinical features include epilepsy and ragged red fibers on muscle biopsy.
Supportive treatment focuses on managing epilepsy.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · MITOCHONDRIAL MYOPATHIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: MITOCHONDRIAL MYOPATHIES (part 2)
items.title: Diagnosing MELAS: Clinical Presentation and Genetic Basis
items.content: MELAS, or Mitochondrial Myopathy, Encephalopathy, Lactic Acidosis, and Stroke-like Episodes, is the most common mitochondrial encephalomyopathy. It often presents before age 20 with seizures and stroke-like episodes that do not follow vascular territories. The A3243G point mutation in tRNA $^{Leu(UUR)}$ occurs in ~80% of cases. Serum lactic acid levels are typically elevated, and neuroimaging may show basal ganglia calcification.
keyPoints: MELAS is the most common mitochondrial encephalomyopathy.
Onset usually before age 20 with seizures and stroke-like episodes.
A3243G point mutation in tRNA $^{Leu(UUR)}$ occurs in ~80% of cases.
Serum lactic acid levels are typically elevated.
Neuroimaging may show basal ganglia calcification.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · MITOCHONDRIAL MYOPATHIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF MUSCLE MEMBRANE EXCITABILITY | CALCIUM CHANNEL DISORDERS OF MUSCLE
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Hypokalemic Periodic Paralysis (HypoKPP)
items.content: Hypokalemic Periodic Paralysis is an autosomal dominant disorder characterized by episodic muscle weakness, often triggered by high-carbohydrate or sodium meals. Diagnosis is confirmed by low serum potassium during attacks, with motor conduction studies showing reduced amplitudes and EMG indicating electrical silence in severely weak muscles.
keyPoints: Hypokalemic Periodic Paralysis (HypoKPP) is autosomal dominant with onset in adolescence.
Episodic weakness often affects proximal limb muscles more than distal, sparing ocular, bulbar, and respiratory muscles.
Weakness may take up to 24 hours to resolve; life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias can occur due to hypokalemia.
Diagnosis is established by low serum potassium during an attack, excluding secondary causes.
Motor conduction studies show reduced amplitudes; EMG shows electrical silence in severely weak muscles.
Approximately 10% of cases are HypoKPP type 2, caused by mutations in the SCN4A gene.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · DISORDERS OF MUSCLE MEMBRANE EXCITABILITY | CALCIUM CHANNEL DISORDERS OF MUSCLE

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF MUSCLE MEMBRANE EXCITABILITY | CALCIUM CHANNEL DISORDERS OF MUSCLE
items.title: Understanding the Genetic Basis of Hypokalemic Periodic Paralysis
items.content: HypoKPP type 1, caused by mutations in the CALCL1A3 gene, is the most common form. Both types of HypoKPP involve abnormal gating pore currents that predispose muscle cells to depolarize when potassium levels are low.
keyPoints: HypoKPP type 1 is caused by mutations in the CALCL1A3 gene.
Approximately 10% of cases are HypoKPP type 2, arising from SCN4A gene mutations.
Mutations lead to abnormal gating pore currents causing muscle cell depolarization when potassium levels are low.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · DISORDERS OF MUSCLE MEMBRANE EXCITABILITY | CALCIUM CHANNEL DISORDERS OF MUSCLE

items.subtopic: Hypokalemic Periodic Paralysis | SODIUM CHANNEL DISORDERS OF MUSCLE
items.title: Management of Hypokalemic Periodic Paralysis (HypoKPP)
items.content: In HypoKPP, severe attacks can be managed with potassium supplementation. Oral KCl is administered at 0.2–0.4 mmol/kg every 30 minutes. IV therapy is rarely needed unless there are complications like swallowing problems or vomiting. Long-term management includes dietary modifications and prophylactic medication.
keyPoints: Oral KCl administration: 0.2–0.4 mmol/kg every 30 min
IV therapy rarely necessary, except with swallowing issues or vomiting
Long-term management involves a low-carbohydrate, low-sodium diet and avoiding intense exercise
Prophylactic acetazolamide or dichlorphenamide can reduce attacks but may exacerbate weakness in HypoKPP type 2
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Hypokalemic Periodic Paralysis | SODIUM CHANNEL DISORDERS OF MUSCLE

items.subtopic: Hypokalemic Periodic Paralysis | SODIUM CHANNEL DISORDERS OF MUSCLE
items.title: Characteristics and Management of Hyperkalemic Periodic Paralysis (HyperKPP)
items.content: HyperKPP is characterized by brief, mild attacks affecting proximal muscles, often precipitated by rest after exercise or fasting. Potassium levels may be normal during attacks. Treatment includes acetazolamide, dichlorphenamide, and possibly mexiletine for myotonia.
keyPoints: Onset usually in the first decade; affects males and females equally
Attacks last 30 min to several hours, affecting proximal muscles but sparing bulbar muscles
Potassium levels may be slightly elevated or normal during attacks
Prophylactic treatment with acetazolamide or dichlorphenamide reduces attack frequency and severity
Mexiletine can help in patients with significant clinical myotonia
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · Hypokalemic Periodic Paralysis | SODIUM CHANNEL DISORDERS OF MUSCLE

items.subtopic: POTASSIUM CHANNEL DISORDERS | CHLORIDE CHANNEL DISORDERS | ENDOCRINE AND METABOLIC MYOPATHIES | THYROID DISORDERS
items.title: Andersen-Tawil Syndrome: Recognize Potassium Channel Disorder with Cardiac and Muscular Implications
items.content: Andersen-Tawil Syndrome is a rare condition marked by episodic weakness, cardiac arrhythmias, and distinct dysmorphic features. It results from mutations in the Kir 2.1 gene, leading to increased muscle cell excitability. The cardiac issues can be life-threatening, including long QT, ventricular ectopy, bidirectional ventricular arrhythmias, and tachycardia. Acetazolamide may help reduce attack frequency and severity.
keyPoints: Characterized by episodic weakness, cardiac arrhythmias, dysmorphic features (short stature, scoliosis, clinodactyly, hypertelorism, small or prominent low-set ears, micrognathia, broad forehead)
Cardiac arrhythmias include long QT, ventricular ectopy, bidirectional ventricular arrhythmias, and tachycardia
Caused by mutations in the Kir 2.1 gene
Acetazolamide may decrease attack frequency and severity
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · POTASSIUM CHANNEL DISORDERS | CHLORIDE CHANNEL DISORDERS | ENDOCRINE AND METABOLIC MYOPATHIES | THYROID DISORDERS

items.subtopic: POTASSIUM CHANNEL DISORDERS | CHLORIDE CHANNEL DISORDERS | ENDOCRINE AND METABOLIC MYOPATHIES | THYROID DISORDERS
items.title: Thyroid Disorders: Hypothyroidism and Hyperthyroidism Impact on Muscle Function
items.content: Hypothyroidism often presents with muscle complaints, including proximal muscle weakness in about one-third of patients. Symptoms include cramps, pain, stiffness, and occasionally enlarged muscles. The serum CK level can be elevated up to 10 times normal, but EMG is typically normal. Muscle biopsy shows no distinctive abnormalities. Hyperthyroidism may cause proximal muscle weakness without myopathic symptoms, with enhanced deep tendon reflexes and possible fasciculations. Hypokalemic periodic paralysis can occur in thyrotoxic patients due to mutations in the KCNJ18 gene.
keyPoints: Hypothyroidism: ~1/3 of patients have proximal muscle weakness; serum CK up to 10 times normal
Muscle cramps, pain, stiffness common; enlarged muscles possible
EMG typically normal; muscle biopsy shows no distinctive abnormalities
Hyperthyroidism: Proximal muscle weakness without myopathic symptoms; enhanced deep tendon reflexes and fasciculations
Hypokalemic periodic paralysis in thyrotoxic patients linked to KCNJ18 gene mutations
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · POTASSIUM CHANNEL DISORDERS | CHLORIDE CHANNEL DISORDERS | ENDOCRINE AND METABOLIC MYOPATHIES | THYROID DISORDERS

items.subtopic: PARATHYROID DISORDERS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 422)
items.title: Hyperparathyroidism and its Muscular Manifestations
items.content: In hyperparathyroidism, proximal muscle weakness, muscle wasting, and brisk reflexes are key features. Neck extensor weakness may occur as part of dropped head syndrome.
keyPoints: Proximal muscle weakness, muscle wasting, and brisk reflexes characterize the condition.
Neck extensor weakness can be a feature (dropped head syndrome).
Serum CK levels are usually normal or slightly elevated.
Elevated serum parathyroid hormone levels with reduced vitamin D and calcium levels.
Muscle biopsies show mild type 2 fiber atrophy.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · PARATHYROID DISORDERS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 422) | ADRENAL DISORDERS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 398) | PITUITARY DISORDERS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 392) | DIABETES MELLITUS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 417)

items.subtopic: ADRENAL DISORDERS (SEE ALSO CHAP. 398)
items.title: Steroid Myopathy in Glucocorticoid Excess
items.content: Glucocorticoid excess leads to steroid myopathy, characterized by proximal muscle weakness and a cushingoid appearance.
keyPoints: Proximal muscle weakness with cushingoid features indicates steroid myopathy.
Serum CK and EMG are typically normal in this condition.
Muscle biopsy reveals type 2b fiber atrophy, though not commonly used for diagnosis.
Primary hyperaldosteronism can cause persistent muscle weakness due to potassium depletion.

items.subtopic: MYOPATHIES OF SYSTEMIC ILLNESS | DRUG-INDUCED OR TOXIC MYOPATHIES | MYOPATHY FROM LIPID-LOWERING AGENTS
items.title: Recognizing Statin Myopathy and Its Management
items.content: Statin myopathy is a common drug-induced condition characterized by muscle toxicity. Patients may present with myalgia, elevated CK levels, or proximal weakness. Concomitant use of statins with fibrates or cyclosporine increases the risk of severe myotoxicity.
keyPoints: Myalgia and elevated CKs are common manifestations.
Severe cases show marked elevations in serum CK (>3–5 times baseline) and may present with myoglobinuria.
Drug cessation usually leads to improvement, though some cases require immunotherapy if an immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy is suspected.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · MYOPATHIES OF SYSTEMIC ILLNESS | DRUG-INDUCED OR TOXIC MYOPATHIES | MYOPATHY FROM LIPID-LOWERING AGENTS | GLUCOCORTICOID-RELATED MYOPATHIES

items.subtopic: MYOPATHIES OF SYSTEMIC ILLNESS | DRUG-INDUCED OR TOXIC MYOPATHIES | GLUCOCORTICOID-RELATED MYOPATHIES
items.title: Understanding Glucocorticoid Myopathy and Its Clinical Implications
items.content: Glucocorticoid myopathy can result from chronic use or high-dose IV administration. Chronic glucocorticoid therapy, especially with prednisone ≥30 mg/d or fluorinated glucocorticoids, leads to proximal weakness and type 2 muscle fiber atrophy.
keyPoints: Chronic glucocorticoid myopathy often presents with normal CK levels but may show low serum potassium.
Acute quadriplegic myopathy can occur with high-dose IV glucocorticoids, leading to severe generalized weakness and ventilatory muscle involvement.
Management involves withdrawal of glucocorticoids and supportive care, with slow recovery.
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · MYOPATHIES OF SYSTEMIC ILLNESS | DRUG-INDUCED OR TOXIC MYOPATHIES | MYOPATHY FROM LIPID-LOWERING AGENTS | GLUCOCORTICOID-RELATED MYOPATHIES

items.subtopic: OTHER DRUG-INDUCED MYOPATHIES | GLOBAL ISSUES
items.title: Identifying Drug-Induced Myopathies: Role of Muscle Biopsy
items.content: Certain drugs, such as amphophilic cationic and antimicrotubular agents, can cause proximal muscle weakness without pain. A muscle biopsy is instrumental in diagnosing these myopathies by revealing autophagic vacuoles.
keyPoints: Drugs implicated: amiodarone, chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, colchicine
Symptoms: painless, proximal muscle weakness
Diagnostic feature on biopsy: prominent autophagic vacuoles
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · OTHER DRUG-INDUCED MYOPATHIES | GLOBAL ISSUES

items.subtopic: OTHER DRUG-INDUCED MYOPATHIES | GLOBAL ISSUES
items.title: Prevalence of Muscular Dystrophies by Ancestry and Geography
items.content: Certain muscular dystrophies show increased prevalence in specific populations. LGMD2A/LGMDR1 is common in Spain, France, Italy, and Great Britain; LGMD2I/LGMDR9 in northern Europe; GNE myopathy in Japan and Ashkenazi Jews; OPMD in Spanish, French-Canadian, and Ashkenazi ancestry.
keyPoints: LGMD2A/LGMDR1: common in Spain, France, Italy, Great Britain
LGMD2I/LGMDR9: more prevalent in northern Europe
GNE myopathy: most common distal myopathy in Japan and Ashkenazi Jews
OPMD: prevalent among Spanish, French-Canadian, Ashkenazi ancestry
items.reference: Muscular Dystrophies and Other Muscle Diseases · OTHER DRUG-INDUCED MYOPATHIES | GLOBAL ISSUES
